channel.title: The Pulse — Health-System AI Daily
channel.description: 0–3 items a day a health-system clinical-AI leader would be embarrassed to miss. De-hyped by contract; quiet days produce no items.

item.title: A fired Mayo Clinic oversight leader alleges the system concealed its AI assistant's 67% error rate
item.description:
Traci Tamiko Eto, Mayo's director of research operations since December 2023 (hired to align the system with federal AI-governance standards), filed a federal retaliation suit July 6 in the District of Minnesota alleging she was demoted and then terminated after flagging AI-compliance failures over 18 months: researchers allegedly deleted unfavorable results to conceal a 67% error rate in Mayo's internal AI assistant MAYA, bypassed IRB review, deployed AI into clinical workflows without authorization, and planned genome-data sharing beyond what patient consents allowed — with ten separate internal whistleblower reports said to have raised similar alarms. Mayo says it embeds privacy, transparency and compliance throughout its AI processes and declined comment on the litigation; these are allegations, not findings. Coverage built from July 9 (Star Tribune, MPR, Becker's, MedCity) through STAT's July 15 roundup — the story broke during the Pulse's missed 7/14–17 window and is reported here as a catch-up.

Why it matters: This is the first high-profile case treating a health system's internal AI-governance process itself as the liability — at the same institution now building a frontier model with Microsoft. Two actions: (1) pressure-test your governance program's escalation path — can a validation lead report an ugly error rate without career risk, and is that path documented in the program, not just assumed? (2) A ready committee and 2027-interview talking point: your kappa/AC1 numbers are credible precisely because they are reportable when they are bad — "AI governance that can't hear bad news is itself the risk." Watch for Mayo's answer to the complaint.

ACT · Module D

item.title: Bipartisan senators demand the denial algorithms from the three biggest Medicare Advantage insurers
item.description:
Sens. Richard Blumenthal (D) and Josh Hawley (R) sent July 14 letters to UnitedHealthcare, Humana, and CVS Health demanding internal records on how each uses AI and predictive software to decide post-acute and rehabilitative-care coverage — an inventory of the algorithms in use, plus confirmation that final denials still cannot be made by technology alone. The probe follows a June HHS Office of Inspector General report finding the three denied post-hospital care at 51–80% rates, far above peers; responses are due July 28. This escalates the AI-denials story from state utilization-review laws and the nH Predict litigation to bipartisan federal oversight of the largest MA plans.

Why it matters: The senators' central demand — proof that a licensed human, not an algorithm, makes the final denial — is the same human-in-the-loop line you build into the steward platform and defend in your validation program. Two ready talking points: (1) the accountability asymmetry — your clinical-AI is held to kappa/AC1 validation while payer denial models face their first federal model-inventory demand; (2) for medical-staff and 2027-interview conversations, "final adjudication stays with a licensed clinician" is now a bipartisan Senate expectation, not just a design preference. Watch the July 28 responses.

WATCH · Module D

item.title: Medicare proposes its first standardized payment lane for clinical-AI software
item.description:
CMS's CY2027 OPPS proposed rule (issued July 2; comments due Aug 31) creates an interim pathway for "Software as a Medical Service" (SaMS) — CMS's rename of algorithm-driven diagnostic software, formerly "SaaS." It designates 36 HCPCS codes as SaMS, moves the 21 separately-paid ones out of clinical APCs into New Technology APCs at roughly CY2026 rates, and adds a new status indicator "O1" (separate payment, no multiple-procedure discount). Named examples span CT-FFR, retinal-image disease detection, ECG-based risk scoring, quantitative brain MRI, and AI prostate-biopsy mapping. CMS calls it an interim "holding pen" pending a durable valuation method for 2028 and flags SaMS acquisition/licensing arrangements as a program-integrity risk.

Why it matters: This is the reimbursement half of the clinical-AI story your governance/validation work usually leaves out. Map your health system's deployed diagnostic-AI tools against the 36-code SaMS list — build-vs-buy and revenue math both change once a diagnostic algorithm is separately billable. Carry the O1 / New-Tech-APC framing to your committee before the Aug 31 comment window; it pairs cleanly with last week's UpDoc note — FDA clearance and Medicare payment for clinical AI are now advancing in parallel.

ACT · Module F

item.title: FDA's first cleared patient-facing LLM is a translator, not a decision-maker
item.description:
UpDoc V1.0, a prescription tool for insulin titration in adults with type 2 diabetes, is the first FDA-cleared device with a patient-facing large language model — 510(k) K253281, granted December 23, 2025, disclosed June 25, with legal and regulatory analyses landing this week. The architecture is the story: the LLM handles only conversation and structured data capture, while dosing logic stays in deterministic, physician-protocolized software (predicate: Hygieia's d-Nav). FDA has now shown exactly how narrowly a patient-facing LLM must be scoped to clear review.

Why it matters: This is the regulatory template for your steward platform's design pattern — LLM shell, deterministic core. Pull the K253281 summary and cite it in your governance/validation framework as FDA's current line on LLM scoping; it is also ready committee language for why your chart-review tool stays decision-support, not a device.

ACT · Module D

item.title: OpenAI is selling a clinician version of ChatGPT directly to hospitals
item.description:
A July 7 Forbes profile details OpenAI's health push: a clinician-specific ChatGPT now rolling out, a waitlisted consumer "ChatGPT Health" tab that connects medical records and wellness apps, and eight health systems — including Cedars-Sinai and HCA — already enterprise customers. No launch dates or pricing disclosed. The counterweight: a February Nature Medicine evaluation found ChatGPT Health under-triaged roughly half of physician-adjudicated emergency scenarios.

Why it matters: Expect two conversations soon — clinicians asking why they can't just use ChatGPT, and ED patients arriving on ChatGPT Health advice. Have a one-paragraph position ready (general-purpose assistant vs validated clinical tool), with the under-triage figure as your evidence anchor; it doubles as governance-committee framing.

WATCH · Module I
